robotics, visual servoing, robot modeling and control, motion planning, sampling-based motion planning, multi-robot coordination, active perception, visual tracking, biomimetic aerial robotics, heterogeneous multi-robot task allocation

## Overview
Seth Hutchinson is a senior researcher and professor of robotics at **Northeastern University**, leading a research group focused on advancing the theory and practice of robot perception, planning, and control. His lab bridges rigorous algorithmic development with real-world robotic systems, emphasizing **visual servoing**, **motion planning**, and multi-robot coordination. Hutchinson's work is distinguished by its blend of foundational textbooks and influential tutorials alongside applied projects (from sensing and tracking to biomimetic flight), and it consistently emphasizes <u>robust perception-to-action</u> pipelines and <u>real-time control</u> for autonomous systems. His publications and textbooks serve as canonical references in the field, and his research agenda places particular value on *principled, implementable methods* that connect theory to practice.

---

## Research Areas
Hutchinson's research spans several core areas of robotics, with sustained contributions to both theory and systems. He is best known for foundational work in **visual servo control** (e.g., "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002), "Visual servo control. I. Basic approaches" (2006), and "Visual servo control. II. Advanced approaches" (2007)), which develops control laws that close the loop on image measurements for manipulation and mobile platforms. Complementing perception-driven control, he has authored influential texts on kinematics and dynamics ("Robot Modeling and Control" (2006); "Robot modeling and control, 2nd edition" (2020)) and coauthored broad treatments of motion planning ("Principles of Robot Motion" (2005)). His work also addresses multi-robot coordination and planning under uncertainty ("Optimal motion planning for multiple robots having independent goals" (2002); "Path planning for permutation-invariant multirobot formations" (2006)), sensing and exploration strategies ("Planning exploration strategies for simultaneous localization and mapping" (2006); "Planning Sensing Strategies in a Robot Work Cell with Multi-Sensor Capabilities" (1989)), and practical performance issues such as real-time tracking and estimator design ("Real-time vision, tracking and control" (2000); "Estimating uncertainty in SSD-based feature tracking" (2002)). More recent and applied contributions include biomimetic flight platforms ("A biomimetic robotic platform to study flight specializations of bats" (2017); "Bat Bot (B2), a biologically inspired flying machine" (2016)) and long-duration autonomy and task-allocation for heterogeneous teams ("Robot ecology: Constraint-based control design for long duration autonomy" (2018); "A resilient and energy-aware task allocation framework for heterogeneous multirobot systems" (2021)).

## Notable Works
- "Robot Modeling and Control" (2006) — a widely used textbook that synthesizes kinematics, dynamics, and control for manipulators and mobile robots; it has become a standard reference for both teaching and research in robot modeling and control, and its later edition (2020) continues to influence curriculum and practice.  
- "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002) — a seminal tutorial paper that established a coherent conceptual framework for image-based and position-based visual servoing, clarifying prerequisites from robotics and computer vision and guiding two decades of follow-on research.  
- "Principles of Robot Motion: Theory, Algorithms, and Implementations" (2005) — a comprehensive treatment of motion planning that connects rigorous theory to algorithmic implementation, widely cited for its role in making planning algorithms accessible and practical for robotic systems.

## Academic Background
Seth Hutchinson has a multi-decade academic career with research and teaching roles spanning leading robotics institutions. Over the years he has collaborated with and published alongside researchers at the University of Illinois and Georgia Tech, and he is currently affiliated with **Northeastern University**, where he continues to supervise students and lead research projects. Hutchinson is the author and coauthor of foundational textbooks and highly cited tutorials that have shaped robotics education and research; his work has accrued over 33,000 citations, reflecting broad influence across control, perception, planning, and multirobot systems. His publication record (from early work on sensing strategies and planning in the late 1980s and 1990s to recent contributions on robotic grasping and long-duration autonomy) demonstrates a sustained impact on both theoretical foundations and real-world robotic applications.

Security, Operating System, Systems, Fuzzing, Concolic execution, Intel SGX, Side-channel attacks, Control-flow integrity, Persistent memory, File systems

## Overview
Taesoo Kim is a faculty member in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on systems and security (see his homepage). His work sits at the intersection of **operating systems**, **software/hardware security**, and practical systems engineering. Kim's group emphasizes building *scalable, deployable* defenses and analysis tools that address real-world threats and performance constraints. Central to his agenda are investigations of <u>trusted execution environments</u>, <u>fuzzing and concolic execution</u>, <u>microarchitectural and side-channel attacks</u>, and <u>performance-conscious OS abstractions for new hardware (e.g., persistent memory, MPK)</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Kim’s research covers a broad range of systems-security topics with a consistent focus on producing practical, high-impact artifacts. He has advanced automated program-analysis and testing through work on hybrid fuzzing and concolic execution (e.g., **QSYM**) and on fuzzing primitives and harnesses for large, real-world software such as JavaScript engines and file systems. A second major thread is the security and hardening of trusted execution environments: multiple papers analyze and mitigate attacks on **Intel SGX** (e.g., branch-shadowing side channels, controlled-channel attacks, and return-oriented programming techniques) and propose defenses (e.g., **T-SGX**, **SGX-shield**). He also develops OS- and hardware-oriented defenses — from system-level countermeasures to cache and memory protection (**STEALTHMEM**, **libmpk**, MPK abstractions) to kernel integrity mechanisms and control-flow integrity refinements. Complementing security defenses, Kim has produced high-performance systems research on storage and memory (e.g., **SplitFS**, persistent-memory index conversion) and on scalability (graph processing on a single machine), showing an integrated interest in both correctness/security and performance.

---

## Notable Works
- "QSYM: A practical concolic execution engine tailored for hybrid fuzzing" (2018) — Introduced an effective concolic execution component designed to be integrated with fuzzers, significantly improving bug-finding power on real-world targets and influencing subsequent hybrid-fuzzing systems.  
- "Inferring fine-grained control flow inside SGX enclaves with branch shadowing" (2017) — Demonstrated practical microarchitectural side-channel techniques to recover enclave control flow, exposing systemic weaknesses in TEEs and motivating defenses and redesigns for secure enclave execution.  
- "STEALTHMEM: System-Level protection against Cache-Based side channel attacks in the cloud" (2012) — Proposed a practical system-level defense to mitigate cache side channels in multi-tenant cloud environments, an early and influential contribution to cross-VM microarchitectural isolation.

---

## Academic Background
Taesoo Kim has an extensive publication record spanning systems, operating systems, and security, with over 12,000 citations across his body of work. Over the past decade-plus he has published in top venues on topics ranging from fuzzing, trusted execution environments, microarchitectural attacks, and OS support for emerging hardware, to scalable data- and storage-systems. At Georgia Tech he supervises student researchers and collaborates widely across the systems and security communities; his projects produce both academic artifacts (papers, tools, datasets) and practical prototypes adopted or referenced by practitioners. His portfolio of highly-cited papers and cross-disciplinary collaborations reflects sustained impact on both defensive techniques (e.g., CFI, data-flow isolation, enclave defenses) and tooling for automated bug finding and diagnosis. For more about his projects, publications, and group members, see his homepage.

software engineering, science policy, articificial intelligence, software reuse, requirements engineering, software design, software maintenance and evolution, software engineering education, software classification and retrieval, genomic variant nomenclature

## Overview
Peter A. Freeman is a senior scholar in computing, based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and formerly on the faculty of the **University of California, Irvine**. He is affiliated with the College of Computing and with research groups focused on **software engineering**, *design theory*, and the intersection of computing with public policy. Freeman’s work spans foundational problems in software *design* and <u>reusability</u>, methods for <u>requirements validation</u>, and broader questions in computing <u>science policy</u> and workforce development. His homepage (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/peter-freeman) lists his publications and highlights a career that combines empirical and conceptual research with policy-oriented analysis.

---

## Research Areas
Freeman’s research program centers on the theory and practice of *software engineering* and the scholarly development of computing as an institutional field. He has contributed deeply to the study of software <u>reusability</u> and classification (see his influential work on *classifying software for reusability*), formal and pragmatic approaches to <u>requirements engineering</u> (including *viewpoint resolution*), and models of <u>design reasoning</u> that connect top‑down and bottom‑up development practices. Over the decades his interests broadened to include computing *education* and curriculum design, the workforce and labor-market implications of IT (e.g., studies on the supply of IT workers), and science‑policy analysis of computing institutions (including historical and organizational work relating to the National Science Foundation). His bibliography also shows interdisciplinary collaborations that touch on applied domains such as bioinformatics tooling and variant nomenclature, reflecting an interest in how software engineering methods transfer to domain‑specific research tools.

## Notable Works
- "Classifying software for reusability" (1987) — a seminal paper that introduced a faceted classification scheme to make software components discoverable and reusable, laying groundwork for later research in component‑based development and software libraries.  
- "Requirements validation through viewpoint resolution" (1991) — introduced the VPR technique for eliciting and reconciling multiple stakeholder viewpoints to improve early validation of complex system requirements.  
- "The Supply of Information Technology Workers in the United States." (1999) — a policy‑oriented study that analyzes IT labor supply and demand, situating workforce issues within their political and educational contexts and informing debates on computing workforce planning.

## Academic Background
Freeman’s publication record, spanning from the early 1970s to the 2010s, indicates a long career in academic computing with faculty appointments at both **UC Irvine** and **Georgia Tech** and sustained engagement with software engineering education and research leadership. His early and highly cited work on design and reuse established him as a central figure in the maturation of software engineering as a discipline; subsequent publications revisit education, methodology, and the institutional foundations of computing. He has bridged disciplinary scholarship and policy analysis, contributing to historical treatments of federal support for computing and studies of the IT workforce. With a total citation count in the high thousands (approximately 4,895 citations across his corpus), Freeman’s contributions have been influential in shaping curricula, research agendas, and policy conversations in computing. His career reflects a blend of research, teaching, mentorship, and service to the broader computing community.
software engineering, science policy, articificial intelligence

Cryptography, security, secure computation, zero knowledge proofs, garbled circuits, oblivious transfer extension, oblivious PRF, private set intersection, privacy-preserving databases, non-interactive zero-knowledge

## Overview
Vladimir Kolesnikov is a faculty researcher in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on *applied cryptography and privacy-preserving systems*. His work centers on practical techniques for secure computation and cryptographic protocols, with particular emphasis on <u>garbled circuits</u>, <u>secure multi-party computation (MPC)</u>, and <u>private set operations</u>. Kolesnikov's research blends theoretical primitives with systems-level implementation, producing both foundational constructions (e.g., optimizations for garbled-circuit evaluation) and scalable systems (e.g., private DBMS and smart-grid protocols) that make *privacy-preserving computation* feasible in practice.

---

## Research Areas
Kolesnikov's research spans the design, analysis, and engineering of cryptographic protocols that enable computation on private data without disclosure. He is a leading contributor to the body of work on <u>garbled-circuit optimizations</u> (introducing and improving techniques such as Free-XOR and later fleXOR), and has developed multiple building blocks that reduce the cost of secure two-party and multi-party protocols—covering gates and arithmetic operations, cut-and-choose strategies, and amortization techniques for repeated executions. His papers also treat efficient primitives that underpin large-scale applications: optimized OT extension and oblivious PRFs for lightweight cryptographic transfers, new paradigms for *private set intersection (PSI)* and *private set union (PSU)* using symmetric-key techniques, and non-interactive zero-knowledge proofs via the MPC-in-the-head paradigm for post-quantum signatures. Complementing these cryptographic advances, Kolesnikov has produced systems-oriented work on scalable private DBMS (Blind Seer), secure evaluation of branching programs with medical applications, and secure protocols tailored to cyber-physical settings such as the smart grid.

---

## Notable Works
- "Improved garbled circuit: Free XOR gates and applications" (2008) — Introduced the *Free-XOR* technique that makes XOR gates essentially cost-free in garbled-circuit protocols, significantly improving practical two-party secure function evaluation and influencing a generation of 2PC implementations.  
- "A pragmatic introduction to secure multi-party computation" (2018) — A widely-cited, accessible survey and roadmap that synthesizes theory and practice in MPC, clarifying design choices and bridging the gap between cryptographic research and applied system development.  
- "Efficient batched oblivious PRF with applications to private set intersection" (2016) — Developed lightweight, batched OPRF protocols that enable practical, high-throughput PSI constructions using symmetric-key techniques, reducing reliance on expensive public-key operations for many real-world private set tasks.

---

## Academic Background
Kolesnikov has been an active researcher in cryptography and secure computation since the early 2000s, authoring influential papers across theory and systems and accruing over 8,700 citations to date. His publication record includes foundational conference and journal contributions as well as patents and collaborative projects with industry research labs (e.g., work tied to Bell Labs / Alcatel-Lucent and other applied research programs). His portfolio demonstrates sustained impact on both the theoretical underpinnings (garbled circuits, OT extension, zero-knowledge) and practical deployments (scalable private DBMS, smart-grid security, and privacy-preserving biometric search). He maintains a lab page and resources for students and collaborators at his homepage (https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~vlad/), and is widely recognized for mentoring graduate students and driving cross-disciplinary work that brings cryptographic techniques into operational systems.
Cryptography, security, secure computation, zero knowledge proofs

Database Systems,Database Design,Schema Integration,Entity-Relationship Modeling,Vertical Partitioning,Distributed Databases,Temporal Databases,Association Rule Mining,Index Selection,Genomic Databases

## Overview
Shamkant B. Navathe is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he has led research and teaching in *database systems* for several decades. He is best known for his role as co‑author of the widely adopted textbook **"Fundamentals of Database Systems"** and for foundational research on <u>conceptual database design</u>, <u>schema integration</u>, <u>data fragmentation and partitioning</u>, and <u>data mining for association rules</u>. His work spans both theoretical foundations and practical system design, bridging *academic research* and *applied database technologies*.

---

## Research Areas
Navathe's research covers a broad spectrum of core database topics informed by his extensive publication record. In the area of *conceptual modeling and design* he advanced entity‑relationship methods and interactive tools for view integration (see "Conceptual database design: an entity‑relationship approach" (1991) and "A tool for integrating conceptual schemas and user views" (1988)). He made influential contributions to *schema integration* and automated reasoning about schemas ("A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration" (1986); "A theory of attributed equivalence..." (2002); "On automatic reasoning for schema integration" (1993)). In *physical and distributed database design* he developed methods and objective functions for vertical partitioning and fragmentation that optimize transaction requirements and distribution costs ("Vertical partitioning algorithms for database design" (1984); "An objective function for vertically partitioning relations..." (1994); "Distribution design of logical database schemas" (2006)). Navathe has also explored *temporal extensions to the relational model* and temporal query systems ("A temporal relational model and a query language" (1989); "Temporal Extensions to the Relational Model and SQL." (1993)), *indexing and automated tuning* ("Adaptive and automated index selection in RDBMS" (1992)), and *data mining*—notably efficient algorithms for mining association rules and negative associations ("An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases" (1995); "Mining for strong negative associations..." (1998)). Beyond core DBMS topics, his work includes applied database efforts such as the MITOMAP mitochondrial genome database (1996, 1998, 2004 updates) and practical concerns like synchronization, version management, and information‑security risk management.

## Notable Works
- "Fundamentals of database systems" (2008) — the comprehensive textbook co‑authored by Navathe that synthesizes *modeling, languages, design, and implementation* of database systems; widely used in curricula and released in multiple editions and translations, cementing his influence on database education.
- "An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases" (1995) — a highly cited contribution in *data mining* that proposed scalable methods for discovering association rules from large transaction databases and influenced subsequent work in pattern discovery and market‑basket analysis.
- "A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration" (1986) — a foundational analysis of approaches to *schema and view integration* across organizational boundaries that shaped later automated and semi‑automated integration methods.

## Academic Background
Shamkant B. Navathe is a long‑standing member of the academic database community and a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**. Over a multi‑decade career he has produced influential research, a best‑selling textbook with multiple international editions and translations (Spanish, Portuguese, German, etc.), and numerous highly cited papers (total citations ≈ **34,522**). His publication record shows sustained contributions to both theoretical foundations and applied systems (including biomedical databases such as MITOMAP and practical patents/industrial collaborations). Navathe has collaborated extensively with leading researchers in the field and his work has been incorporated into graduate and undergraduate database curricula worldwide, reflecting broad professional recognition even where specific awards are not listed here.
Professor of Computer Science, Georgia Institute of Technology

human-computer interaction, information visualization, visual analytics, sensemaking, analytic provenance, semantic interaction, interactive machine learning, mixed-initiative visual analytics, cognitive bias mitigation, natural language interfaces

## Overview
Alex Endert is an **Associate Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group within the Georgia Tech Visual Analytics Lab (see http://va.gatech.edu/endert). His work centers on **visual analytics**, *human–computer interaction*, and **information visualization**, with a particular emphasis on enabling and studying analytic reasoning through interactive systems. Across his research he investigates how people and algorithms can collaborate during sensemaking, focusing on <u>interactive model steering</u>, <u>provenance-aware analysis</u>, and <u>display and interaction designs for complex analytic workspaces</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Endert’s scholarship spans core topics in **visual analytics** and **information visualization**, often bridging algorithmic models and human interaction. He has explored how physical and virtual workspaces support cognition (e.g., large, high-resolution displays and spatial organization in “Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking”), how interaction can implicitly steer models and represent users’ analytical reasoning (e.g., the family of *semantic interaction* papers such as “Semantic interaction for visual text analytics” and “Semantic interaction for sensemaking”), and how provenance and analytic history can be characterized and used to improve reproducibility and interpretability (see “Characterizing provenance in visualization and data analysis”). More recently, his work examines the integration of **machine learning** into interactive visual analytics pipelines (“The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics”), methods for surfacing automated insights and facts to aid interpretation, and mechanisms to detect or mitigate human cognitive biases during interactive analysis. Across these topics he combines user studies, interaction design, and algorithmic innovations to support <u>human-centered, mixed-initiative</u> analytic workflows.

## Notable Works
- “Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking” (2010) — seminal empirical and design work demonstrating how **large, high-resolution displays** and spatial externalization support complex sensemaking tasks, influencing subsequent research on display-based workspaces and co-located collaboration.  
- “Characterizing provenance in visualization and data analysis: an organizational framework of provenance types and purposes” (2015) — a foundational framework that organizes how provenance has been conceptualized and used in visualization and analytics, shaping how systems track, present, and leverage analytic history.  
- “The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics” (2017) — a comprehensive review and synthesis that articulates principles, challenges, and directions for coupling **machine learning** with interactive visualization to promote effective human–machine analytic partnerships.

## Academic Background
Alex Endert is an established researcher in the HCI and visualization communities and an Associate Professor at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing, where he directs work in the Visual Analytics Lab. His publication record includes numerous highly cited conference and journal papers (total citations: **7,616**) that appear in leading venues of **visualization**, **HCI**, and **visual analytics**. He regularly contributes to community directions on topics such as mixed-initiative analysis, interactive machine learning, analytic provenance, and bias-aware visualization. Endert mentors graduate students and collaborates widely across academia, industry, and government, producing both foundational theory and prototype systems that have advanced understanding of how interactive visual interfaces can couple human cognition with computational models.
human-computer interaction, information visualization, visual analytics
Cybersecurity, AI, Blockchain, DSP, Computers, Internet of Things, Cloud computing, Smart contracts, Blockchain scalability, Identity and access management

## Overview
Vijay Madisetti is a Professor of **Cybersecurity & Privacy** at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a multidisciplinary research group that bridges **electrical and computer engineering**, *security*, and *applied data science*. His work spans foundational and translational research, with particular emphasis on <u>blockchain-enabled systems</u>, <u>industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)</u>, and <u>digital signal processing (DSP)</u>. Madisetti’s portfolio includes widely used reference texts, high-impact systems papers, and patents that address the intersection of **distributed ledgers**, **secure data management**, and real-time signal processing for embedded and cloud-scale applications.

---

## Research Areas
Madisetti’s research program integrates theory, system design, and practical deployment across several complementary areas. In the area of distributed ledgers and financial/identity systems he has developed methods for tuning blockchain performance and interoperability (see patents such as "Method and system for tuning blockchain scalability for fast and low-cost payment and transaction processing" and "Method and system for blockchain-based combined identity, ownership, integrity and custody management"), and a seminal systems treatment of ledger technologies for manufacturing and operations in "Blockchain platform for industrial internet of things" (2016). His longstanding work in *digital signal processing* is reflected in authoritative handbooks and textbooks (e.g., "Digital Signal Processing Handbook" and "VLSI digital signal processors"), and in signal-processing theory pieces such as "Cyclostationary signal analysis" and work on the discrete Radon transform. He has also advanced cloud-based analytics and systems for large-scale sensing and healthcare — for example, proposing cloud-native approaches to interoperable electronic health records ("A cloud-based approach for interoperable electronic health records (EHRs)") and frameworks for analyzing massive machine maintenance data in the cloud ("Analyzing massive machine maintenance data in a computing cloud"). Across these topics he emphasizes scalable, secure, and interoperable designs that connect low-level DSP/VLSI techniques to high-level distributed and cloud architectures.

## Notable Works
- "Blockchain platform for industrial internet of things" (2016) — a high-impact systems paper that articulates how **blockchain** can be architected and deployed to secure and coordinate IIoT applications, driving subsequent work on ledger-enabled industrial automation and provenance.  
- "Digital Signal Processing Handbook" (2010) — an authoritative reference consolidating theoretical and applied DSP techniques used across communications, audio, radar, and imaging, and reflecting Madisetti’s deep influence in signal-processing education and practice.  
- "A cloud-based approach for interoperable electronic health records (EHRs)" (2013) — a practical framework that combines cloud infrastructure with data-interoperability design to address the challenges of healthcare information exchange and analytics at scale.

## Academic Background
Vijay Madisetti is a long-standing faculty member at Georgia Tech and is recognized as a **Fellow of the IEEE** for his contributions spanning DSP, embedded systems, and secure distributed systems. Over his career he has authored and edited multiple textbooks and handbooks, contributed foundational theory, and been listed as inventor on numerous patents advancing blockchain performance, identity management, and secure cloud services. His publication record includes highly cited books, conference papers, journal articles, and industry-facing patents, amounting to over 8,500 citations — a reflection of both deep disciplinary impact in *digital signal processing* and influential cross-disciplinary work in **IoT**, **cloud computing**, and **blockchain-enabled security**. He maintains an active research and teaching presence at Georgia Tech and on his lab homepage (http://www.ece.gatech.edu/~vkm), mentoring students and collaborating with academic and industrial partners on applied cybersecurity and systems problems.
Professor of Cybersecurity & Privacy, Georgia Tech

Randomized Algorithms, Discrete Probability, Machine Learning, Glauber dynamics, Spectral independence, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), Mixing times of Markov chains, Spin systems (Ising/hardcore/Potts), Approximate counting and sampling, Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC)

## Overview
Zongchen Chen is a faculty researcher based at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an active research group focused on the theory and practice of randomized algorithms and high-dimensional sampling. His work centers on rigorous analysis of *Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)* methods and probabilistic models, with particular emphasis on <u>Glauber dynamics</u>, <u>entropy factorization</u>, and <u>mixing time</u> bounds. Across a body of work that spans both discrete spin systems and continuous log-concave sampling, Chen develops and refines tools—such as **spectral independence**, **contraction-based analyses**, and stochastic-localization-like techniques—that deliver near-optimal convergence guarantees and algorithmic consequences for sampling, counting, and hypothesis testing.

---

## Research Areas
Chen’s research lies at the intersection of **randomized algorithms**, *discrete probability*, and **machine learning**, with a strong emphasis on the algorithmic and analytic foundations of sampling and inference. Recurring technical themes in his papers include the development and application of <u>spectral independence</u> and high-dimensional expansion to prove rapid mixing of single-site and block dynamics (see “Optimal Mixing of Glauber Dynamics: Entropy Factorization via High-Dimensional Expansion” and “Rapid mixing for colorings via spectral independence”), contraction techniques to extend rapid-mixing results up to the *uniqueness threshold* for antiferromagnetic 2-spin systems (“Rapid mixing of Glauber dynamics up to uniqueness via contraction”), and sharp analyses of continuous-time samplers such as **Hamiltonian Monte Carlo** in strongly log-concave settings (“Optimal Convergence Rate of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Strongly Logconcave Distributions”). He also pursues algorithmic questions beyond classical spin systems—covering hardness and identity testing for graphical models, structure learning for H-colorings, sampling/counting on random graphs and random k-SAT, fast algorithms at low temperatures, and combinatorial MCMC for matchings and edge covers—showing a sustained interest in both positive algorithmic results (FPRAS, nearly-linear sampling algorithms) and lower bounds/limitations for local processes (Metropolis, simulated annealing).

---

## Notable Works
- “Optimal Mixing of Glauber Dynamics: Entropy Factorization via High-Dimensional Expansion” (2021) — establishes O(n log n) mixing time bounds for single-site Glauber dynamics across a range of settings by combining **entropy factorization** with high-dimensional expansion and a refined spectral independence framework, yielding near-optimal convergence guarantees for many spin systems.  
- “Rapid mixing of Glauber dynamics up to uniqueness via contraction” (2023) — introduces a contraction-based approach that proves rapid mixing for general antiferromagnetic 2-spin systems up to the tree-uniqueness threshold, unifying and extending prior uniqueness-region sampling results for models such as the hardcore and antiferromagnetic Ising models.  
- “Optimal Convergence Rate of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Strongly Logconcave Distributions” (2022) — proves improved bounds on the relaxation time (spectral gap inverse) of ideal HMC for strongly log-concave targets, sharpening prior analyses and bridging discrete spin-system techniques with continuous-sampling theory.

---

## Academic Background
Zongchen Chen’s publication record spans leading theory venues and journals in theoretical computer science, probability, and statistics, reflecting a research trajectory rooted in rigorous analysis of randomized algorithms and probabilistic models. With a broad coauthor network and 915 citations to date, his work has influenced contemporary approaches to spectral independence, entropy-based factorization, and algorithmic sampling at and beyond phase-transition thresholds. Earlier publications indicate a capacity for cross-disciplinary work (including distributed and data-center systems in 2015–2018), while his more recent corpus focuses tightly on theoretical advances in MCMC, sampling, and inference for graphical models. Chen is affiliated with Georgia Tech and maintains an up-to-date research homepage that aggregates his papers, code, and preprints; his outputs have produced a mixture of constructive algorithmic results (FPRAS, nearly-linear sampling) and matching hardness or lower-bound insights that shape ongoing work on the limits of local sampling procedures.
Randomized Algorithms, Discrete Probability, Machine Learning
